symbols: lily held by Gabriel — purity and the virginal conception
large wings — the angelic nature and heavenly origin of the messenger
Mary's startled gesture — her initial alarm at the angelic greeting, 'greatly troubled' by his words
open book beside Mary — she was in prayer or reading Scripture when interrupted by the divine message
dark setting with golden light — the divine breaking into ordinary human life
description: A dramatic composition split between two halves. At left, a powerful winged angel in a blue robe with golden-bronze underlining stands on a cloud, holding a white lily in his right hand. His large feathered wings spread behind him. At right, a woman in a mauve-pink dress and white head covering sits or kneels, her hands raised in a gesture of surprise or alarm. An open book rests beside her. The background is dark with golden light emanating around the angel. The two figures face each other across the dark space.
theological_description: Van de Velde's Annunciation depicts the defining moment of Christian history — the angel Gabriel's appearance to Mary with the news that she would conceive and bear the Son of God (Luke 1:26-38). Gabriel's greeting, 'Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with you,' troubled Mary deeply (Luke 1:29). The angel's reassurance — 'Do not be afraid, Mary; you have found favor with God' — was followed by the world-changing announcement: 'You will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High' (Luke 1:30-32). Mary's startled gesture captures her human response to this overwhelming revelation. The lily Gabriel holds symbolizes the virginal conception that Isaiah prophesied: 'The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel' (Isaiah 7:14). Mary's response — 'I am the Lord's servant. May your word to me be fulfilled' (Luke 1:38) — is the supreme act of human faith and obedience. In that moment, as the Church Fathers taught, the Word became flesh (John 1:14) — God entered the material world through the willing consent of a young woman. The Annunciation is the hinge of all history: the eternal enters time, the Creator enters creation, the divine assumes humanity.
